title: Writing Expressions
description: Turn words into algebra expressions without losing what the words mean.

scaffolded.title: Turn words into algebra
scaffolded.body: Word problems are math in disguise. Name the unknown **x**, then turn key words into symbols.

steps.text: A word problem often hides a number inside the words.

gate.template: Whenever a problem says "a number," name it ___.
gate.hint: When you don't know a number yet, give it a short letter label to stand in for it.

steps.text: Translate the action words into symbols.

gate.hint: Plus is the word for putting amounts together, and minus is the word for taking an amount away.
The word times means making several equal copies of an amount, not just putting two amounts together.

scaffolded.prompt: "A number plus 5" can be written as...
hints: Name the unknown number with a letter first.
Use x for the number, then let plus 5 pick the operation.
The word plus tells you to add 5 to your x.

feedback.correct: Yes! Call it x, and "plus" adds 5: x + 5.
incorrect: Start with x for the unknown number.

scaffolded.prompt: "Three times a number" can be written as...
hints: The word times tells you to multiply, not add.
Three times a number means three copies of x.
Write copies compactly by putting the count in front of x.

feedback.correct: Exactly, "three times" means three copies, written 3x.
incorrect: When you multiply, the number sits in front of the variable.

scaffolded.prompt: Story: "a number plus 4 equals 10." On the scale, x plus 4 balances 10. Find the number, keep both pans balanced!

followUpMc.prompt: What number made the story true?

feedback.correct: Yes! Translate, balance, solve, the number is 6.
incorrect: x is alone now. Read the remaining weight.

hints: Turn the words into an equation before you solve.
The story becomes x plus 4 equals 10, so undo that plus 4.
Take 4 off both pans, then read what x balances.

feedback.correct: Nice, the story became x + 4 = 10, and x = 6.
incorrect: Keep the scale balanced by changing both sides.

scaffolded.title: Build it in pieces
scaffolded.body: Longer phrases have more parts. Build the expression one piece at a time, in word order.

steps.text: Take "four more than twice a number." Start with "twice a number."

gate.template: "Twice a number" means two copies, written ___.
gate.hint: Twice means two copies, so write how many copies, two, right in front of a single x.
The words four more mean you are putting an extra 4 on top of what you already have.

steps.text: Snap the pieces together in word order.

gate.template: In order, "four more than twice a number" is ___.
gate.hint: Build it in pieces, write the two-copies part first, then tack the extra 4 on at the end.

scaffolded.prompt: Build the expression for: "two more than three times a number."
hints: This phrase has two parts, so build them in order.
Start with three times a number, the multiply part.
Then tack on two more by adding 2 at the end.

incorrect: Build it in order: three times a number, then two more.
Translate in pieces: the times part first, then add two more.
Which operation does the word "minus" tell you to use?
Take it one piece at a time: the "times" part, then the "plus" part.

followUp.prompt: What if it said "two times a number minus 1" instead? How would the expression change?
followUp.why: The word minus makes that last piece a subtraction, so the + 1 turns into - 1 while 2x stays the same.

mastery.prompt: Which expression means "5 more than a number"?

incorrect: Does "more than" tell you to add or subtract?

followUp.prompt: What if it said "5 times a number" instead of "5 more than a number"? How would the expression change?
followUp.why: Times means equal copies, so you multiply to get 5x instead of adding to get x + 5.
